user_reply: Fundamentally, people want to minimize coordination tax: they need to ask for a specific outcome in a way that can be answered quickly without meetings.
The fundamental motivation is to reduce coordination tax by compressing each request into a single, explicit question.
The load-bearing assumption is that senders will accept a small upfront cost (about 20-30 seconds) to formulate that one question because it saves them multiple back-and-forth messages later.

First-party: I personally conducted 15 semi-structured interviews and ran a two-week internal pilot; direct quotes include 'I spend ~6 hours weekly chasing clarifications' and 'when the request had one clear question, I answered immediately'.

Scale: 15 interviews across 3 teams; pilot had 48 active users who created 312 requests over 14 days; 237 received a first reply within 2 hours (76%), and 79% of active users opted into the single-question composer by day 5.
In our pilot, enforcing a single explicit question increased first-response rate from 41% (baseline chat) to 76% across 312 requests in two weeks.

Directionally aligned: senders spent a median +18 seconds composing, but recipients saved a median 2.7 minutes per request; drop-off was low (7% abandoned drafts), and the top reason was 'unclear objective', which the composer aims to reduce.

One-hour ship: a message composer with a required 'Question' field and an optional 140-character context, plus a simple inbox that lets recipients reply with a single text box; no voice, no locks, no reminders.

Possible side-effects: senders might split complex asks into multiple messages and recipients might feel pressured to answer quickly; we will watch for fragmentation (multi-thread bursts) and include a lightweight 'decline with reason' so recipients can push back on poor questions.
